y <- 14593

title: Week 1 Answers (0703)
text:
4 Read CSV

Download the dataset (right click here) and save it in the data folder.
Install and call the package tidyverse.
Read earthquakes.csv and save it as earthquakes_raw.
Take a look of earthquakes_raw by clicking it from Rstudio the enviornment.

install.packages("tidyverse")
library(tidyverse)

earthquakes_raw <- read.csv("data/earthquakes.csv")

title: Homework 1 Answers
text:
1 Computer Exercises
(census.csv)
# clear the memory and import package
rm(list = ls())
library(tidyverse)

# import data
census <- read.csv("census.csv")

1.1 Summary Statistics Table

summary_table <- census %>%
  summarise(
    variable = c("total_personal_income", "total_family_income", "age"),
    mean = c(
      mean(total_personal_income, na.rm = TRUE),
      mean(total_family_income, na.rm = TRUE),
      mean(age, na.rm = TRUE)
    ),
    sd = c(
      sd(total_personal_income, na.rm = TRUE),
      sd(total_family_income, na.rm = TRUE),
      sd(age, na.rm = TRUE)
    ),
    min = c(
      min(total_personal_income, na.rm = TRUE),
      min(total_family_income, na.rm = TRUE),
      min(age, na.rm = TRUE)
    ),
    q25 = c(
      quantile(total_personal_income, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
      quantile(total_family_income, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
      quantile(age, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
    ),
    median = c(
      median(total_personal_income, na.rm = TRUE),
      median(total_family_income, na.rm = TRUE),
      median(age, na.rm = TRUE)
    ),
    q75 = c(
      quantile(total_personal_income, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
      quantile(total_family_income, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
      quantile(age, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
    ),
    max = c(
      max(total_personal_income, na.rm = TRUE),
      max(total_family_income, na.rm = TRUE),
      max(age, na.rm = TRUE)
    )
  )

summary_table[, 2:8] <- round(summary_table[, 2:8], 2)
summary_table

               variable     mean       sd   min   q25 median   q75    max
1 total_personal_income 29081.72 46089.29 -4400  5900  17750 37000 456000
2   total_family_income 57410.98 70732.06     0 21500  43000 70700 892050
3                   age    35.30    21.81     0    17     35    51     93




1.2 Distribution of Total Personal Income
census_income <- census[!is.na(census$total_personal_income), ]

ggplot(census_income, aes(x = total_personal_income)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 10000, fill = "steelblue", color = "black") +
  labs(
    x = "Total Personal Income",
    y = "Frequency",
    title = "Distribution of Total Personal Income"
  ) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))









The distribution of total personal income is strongly right-skewed. Most observations are concentrated in the lower income ranges, especially from 0 to about 40{,}000. There are also several high-income observations, with the maximum equal to 456{,}000, which appear as potential outliers.


1.3 Age and Total Personal Income
ggplot(census_income, aes(x = age, y = total_personal_income)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    x = "Age",
    y = "Total Personal Income",
    title = "Age and Total Personal Income"
  ) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))









There appears to be a weak positive relationship between age and personal income. The points are widely scattered, so the association is not strong.


1.4 Personal Income by Sex
ggplot(census_income, aes(x = sex, y = total_personal_income)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Total Personal Income",
    title = "Total Personal Income by Sex"
  ) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))









The male group has a higher median personal income. The male group also exhibits greater overall variability, with a wider interquartile range and more extreme high-income observations.


1.5 Sex and Race Contingency Table
sex_race_table <- table(census$sex, census$race_general)
sex_race_table_withsum <- addmargins(sex_race_table)
sex_race_table_withsum


        
         American Indian or Alaska Native Black Chinese Japanese Other
  Female                                2    32       6        0    12
  Male                                  1    39       2        1    13
  Sum                                   3    71       8        1    25
        
         Other Asian or Pacific Islander Two major races White Sum
  Female                               5               5   170 232
  Male                                 8              11   193 268
  Sum                                 13              16   363 500


ggplot(census, aes(x = race_general, fill = sex)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    x = "General Race",
    y = "Headcount",
    fill = "Sex",
    title = "Headcount by Sex and Race"
  ) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5))









The largest race category in this sample is White for both sexes. The bar plot shows that male and female counts are broadly similar within most race categories, though males are slightly more numerous in the full sample.

title: Homework 1 Answers
text:
2 Textbook Exercises

2.1 Question 1.4

The research question is: “Do asthmatic patients who practice the Buteyko method experience improvement in their condition?”
The cases are 600 adult patients aged 18–69 years diagnosed and currently treated for asthma.
The variables are whether or not the patient practiced the Buteyko method (categorical) and measures of quality of life, activity, asthma symptoms, and medication reduction of the patients (categorical, ordinal). It may also be reasonable to treat the ratings on a scale of 0 to 10 as discrete numerical variables.



2.2 Question 1.10

Each row of the data matrix represents a participant in the survey.
There are 1,691 participants in the survey.
The table below lists the types of variables included in the study:




Variable
Type




sex
categorical


age
numerical, continuous


maritalStatus
categorical


grossIncome
ordinal*


smoke
categorical


amtWeekends
numerical, discrete


amtWeekdays
numerical, discrete



* Note that income is a continuous numerical variable, but in this survey, it is recorded as an ordinal variable.


2.3 Question 1.22

Observational.

No. Because the study is observational, we cannot infer causation.

Possible confounders: caffeine intake and lack of sleep. Either could explain the observed association between stress and muscle cramps (e.g., students may cramp due to increased caffeine use or insufficient sleep).



2.4 Question 1.38

Simple random sampling: effective – gives every household equal probability of selection.

Stratified sampling: effective – neighborhoods are distinct; stratifying ensures sampling from each neighborhood.

Cluster sampling: not effective – sampling whole neighborhoods would miss households from some areas, and neighborhoods differ greatly.

Multi-stage sampling: suffers from the same issue as (c).

Convenience sampling: not effective – yields a biased sample (e.g., only nearby/similar neighborhoods; misses distant ones).



2.5 Question 1.40

Explanatory variable: percent of the population with a bachelor’s degree.
Response variable: per-capita income (in thousands).

Relationship: strong positive linear association – as the bachelor’s-degree percent increases, per-capita income tends to increase. Few counties exceed 60% bachelor’s degree or $50k per-capita income.

Causality: observational study; no causal claim. We can only say higher bachelor’s-degree share is associated with higher per-capita income.



2.6 Question 2.12
Median is around 80. Because the distribution is left-skewed, the mean is expected to be slightly lower than the median.


2.7 Question 2.30

If \dfrac{\bar{x}}{\mathrm{median}} = 1, then \bar{x}=\mathrm{median}. This matches symmetric distributions.

If \dfrac{\bar{x}}{\mathrm{median}} < 1, then \bar{x}<\mathrm{median}. This matches left-skewed distributions (lower values pull the mean down).

If \dfrac{\bar{x}}{\mathrm{median}} > 1, then \bar{x}>\mathrm{median}. This matches right-skewed distributions (higher values pull the mean up).
